task: Compare what Git tracks well in ML projects versus what it does not efficiently track.

content.message: Task received. Retrieve evidence from the course document before answering.

content.input: Compare what Git tracks well in ML projects versus what it does not efficiently track.

results.preview: What is Git? (cont.) Git is a distributed version control system that tracks changes to files over time. For ML projects, Git tracks: • Code changes to training scripts and inference services • Configuration file updates
Git’s Three States (cont.) ￿ See: Git Fundamentals for MLOps 20

Essential Git Commands (cont.) # Initialize and clone gitinit gitclone https://github.com/username/repo.git # Stage and commit gitadd . gitcommit-m"Add prediction endpoint" # Branch and merge gitcheckout -bfeature-branch

ML vs LLM Comparison (cont.) Aspect T raditional ML LLM Systems Model source T rained in-house Pre-trained via API Data requirements Labeled training data Prompts, few-shot examples Customization Feature engineering Prom

content.message: Enough evidence retrieved. Use reasoning tool to synthesize the final answer.

input.task: Compare what Git tracks well in ML projects versus what it does not efficiently track.

content.answer_preview: Git efficiently tracks code changes to training scripts and inference services, configuration file updates (Y AML, JSON), documentation revisions, and dependency specifications within ML projects. However, it does not effectively track large model weights or datasets. For managing these larger files, tools like DVC or Git LFS are recommended (Page 21, Chunk 20).

final_answer: Git efficiently tracks code changes to training scripts and inference services, configuration file updates (Y AML, JSON), documentation revisions, and dependency specifications within ML projects. However, it does not effectively track large model weights or datasets. For managing these larger files, tools like DVC or Git LFS are recommended (Page 21, Chunk 20).
